title: Extended Operation OPSEC Framework
when_to_use: Use Extended Operation OPSEC Framework to develop professional-grade extended operation opsec framework capabilities at Mastery level. This recipe is part of the Adversary Infrastructure at Scale pillar and integrates with linked missions OM-M06, OM-M07.

steps.title: OPSEC Threat Model
steps.instruction: Develop threat model for extended operation: Who is looking (defenders, law enforcement, competitors)? What are they looking for (malware signatures, C2 traffic, team members)? Consequences: engagement impact, operator safety, platform exposure. Rate threats by likelihood and impact. Produce risk matrix with heat map. Example: "Detected team server" is high likelihood + high impact = highest risk.
steps.tip: Threat model should be operation-specific. Different adversaries (corporate SOC vs nation-state) have different detection capabilities.
steps.expected_output: OPSEC threat model with risk matrix

steps.title: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)
steps.instruction: Document comprehensive SOPs covering: daily operations (C2 usage, logs review, anomaly detection), infrastructure management (team server updates, certificate rotation, monitoring), inter-operator communications (secure channels, code words, authentication), incident response initiation. Each SOP: clear steps, decision trees, abort conditions, documentation requirements.
steps.tip: SOPs should be so detailed that a new operator can follow them. Include "what if" scenarios.

steps.title: OPSEC Audit Framework
steps.instruction: Design audit system: daily checklists for operators (infrastructure status, C2 behavioral review, communication log review), weekly reviews (anomaly analysis, threat intelligence digest), automated monitoring (certificate expiration, domain reputation, traffic patterns). Framework: checklist format, evidence collection, audit logging.
steps.tip: Audits catch problems before they become catastrophes. Automated monitoring + human review is most effective.
steps.expected_output: Audit framework with templates and checklists

steps.title: OPSEC Incident Response Playbook
steps.instruction: Design OPSEC-specific incident response: detection (infrastructure discovered, operator identified, malware discovered), assessment (scope of compromise, operator exposure, engagement impact), containment (burn infrastructure, change credentials, operator exfil), and decision tree (abort? pivot? escalate?). Include: communication protocols, backup channels, escalation criteria.
steps.tip: OPSEC IR differs from regular IR; focus is operator safety and engagement survival.
steps.expected_output: OPSEC IR playbook with decision trees

steps.title: Tabletop Exercise
steps.instruction: Conduct realistic tabletop: simulate major OPSEC failure (e.g., malware sample leaked, team server burned, operator phone compromised) during active engagement. Facilitate exercise: present scenario, have team execute OPSEC IR playbook, note decision quality, measure response time. Document: what worked, what failed, lessons learned.
steps.tip: Tabletop exercises are invaluable training. Real stress reveals gaps in procedures.
steps.expected_output: Tabletop scenario + facilitator guide + after-action report

steps.title: OPSEC Training Material
steps.instruction: Create onboarding material for new operators: training presentation (slides), assessment questions, case studies of OPSEC failures, decision trees for common scenarios. Training should cover: threat model, SOP walkthroughs, incident response procedures, communication protocols, behavioral OPSEC (tradecraft). Include real-world examples (with sanitization).
steps.tip: Training transforms OPSEC from policy to culture. Repetition builds muscle memory.
steps.expected_output: Training deck + assessment questions + case studies
